questions.question: What is the maximum limit of alcohol consumption to define non-alcoholic fatty liver disease?
options: 10 g/day in women and 20 g/day in men
40 g/day in women and 50 g/day in men
30 g/day in women and 40 g/day in men
20 g/day in women and 30 g/day in men
questions.explanation: For the diagnosis of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, significant alcohol consumption is excluded, defined as >20 g/day in women and >30 g/day in men, according to international guidelines.

questions.question: What type of hepatic steatosis is considered in the definition of non-alcoholic fatty liver?
questions.explanation: Hepatic steatosis in non-alcoholic fatty liver disease can be macrovesicular or microvesicular, with macrovesicular being the most common; both are included in the histological definition.

questions.question: What is the main criterion for excluding alcohol in the diagnosis of non-alcoholic fatty liver?
options: Weekly consumption less than 140 g in women
Daily consumption of less than 10 g in both sexes
Total absence of alcohol consumption in life
History of alcohol consumption in the last 10 years
questions.explanation: The main criterion is a weekly consumption of less than 140 g in women and 210 g in men, or its daily equivalent, to rule out alcoholic liver disease and define non-alcoholic liver disease.

questions.question: What percentage of hepatic steatosis is required for the histological diagnosis of nonalcoholic fatty liver?
questions.explanation: The histological diagnosis of nonalcoholic fatty liver requires hepatic steatosis in at least 5% of hepatocytes, according to the simple hepatic steatosis classification.

questions.question: Which of the following conditions excludes the diagnosis of nonalcoholic fatty liver disease?
questions.explanation: Chronic hepatitis C (especially genotype 3) can cause hepatic steatosis and should be excluded for the diagnosis of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, along with other secondary causes.

questions.question: What threshold of daily alcohol consumption defines exclusion in men for non-alcoholic fatty liver disease?
questions.explanation: In men, daily consumption of more than 30 g of alcohol (equivalent to ~3 units) is considered significant and excludes the diagnosis of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease.

questions.question: What is the essential histological characteristic of nonalcoholic steatohepatitis?
questions.explanation: Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis is defined by the presence of steatosis, ballooning of hepatocytes and lobular inflammation, unlike simple steatosis which only presents fat accumulation.

questions.question: What pathophysiological factor is key in the transition from steatosis to steatohepatitis?
questions.explanation: Lipotoxicity of free fatty acids and oxidative stress generate mitochondrial damage and an inflammatory response, triggering the progression from simple steatosis to steatohepatitis.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for non-alcoholic steatohepatitis?
questions.explanation: Vitamin E (800 IU/day) is indicated in non-diabetic patients with steatohepatitis confirmed by biopsy, as it reduces oxidative stress and improves liver histology.

questions.question: What non-invasive test is used to estimate fibrosis in steatohepatitis?
questions.explanation: Transient liver elastography (FibroScan) measures liver stiffness non-invasively and is the most validated tool to estimate fibrosis in patients with steatohepatitis.

questions.question: Which of the following diagnostic criteria differentiates steatohepatitis from simple steatosis?
options: Presence of ballonization and Mallory bodies
Elevation of transaminases greater than 5 times
questions.explanation: Hepatocellular ballooning and Mallory bodies are distinctive histological findings of nonalcoholic steatohepatitis, not present in simple steatosis.

questions.question: What is the main metabolic risk factor for developing steatohepatitis?
questions.explanation: Insulin resistance is the central pathophysiological axis in nonalcoholic fatty liver disease, favoring lipolysis and hepatic accumulation of fatty acids.

questions.question: Which ultrasound finding is characteristic of nonalcoholic fatty liver?
questions.explanation: On ultrasound, nonalcoholic fatty liver manifests as a diffuse increase in hepatic echogenicity compared to the renal parenchyma, known as hyperechogenic liver.

questions.question: What is the main ultrasound criterion for the diagnosis of nonalcoholic steatohepatitis?
questions.explanation: Posterior attenuation of the ultrasound beam is a sign of moderate to severe steatosis, indicating diffuse fatty infiltration that makes sound penetration difficult.

questions.question: What structure is used as a reference to compare echogenicity in fatty liver?
questions.explanation: Hepatic echogenicity is compared with that of the right renal parenchyma; In fatty liver, the liver becomes more echogenic than the kidney, which is a key sign.

questions.question: What ultrasound sign suggests severe hepatic steatosis?
questions.explanation: In severe steatosis, diffuse fatty infiltration reduces visualization of the intrahepatic hepatic vessels, especially the suprahepatic and portal veins.

questions.question: What differential diagnosis should be considered when faced with heterogeneous hepatic echogenicity?
questions.explanation: Focal fatty liver may present irregular hyperechoic areas that simulate occupying lesions; It is differentiated by the absence of mass effect and geographical distribution.

questions.question: Which ultrasound finding rules out significant hepatic steatosis?
questions.explanation: A normal hepatic echogenicity, comparable to or less than that of the right kidney, rules out significant hepatic steatosis, since fat increases echogenicity.

questions.question: What is the most used histological classification in liver biopsy for non-alcoholic fatty liver?
questions.explanation: The Brunt classification is the most used to evaluate steatosis, lobular inflammation and hepatocellular ballooning in liver biopsy for non-alcoholic fatty liver.

questions.question: What histological finding is characteristic of nonalcoholic steatohepatitis on liver biopsy?
questions.explanation: Hepatocellular ballooning, along with macrovesicular steatosis and lobular inflammation, is the hallmark finding of nonalcoholic steatohepatitis on liver biopsy.

questions.question: Which of the following parameters is NOT evaluated in the Brunt classification for non-alcoholic fatty liver?
questions.explanation: Punch necrosis is characteristic of autoimmune or viral hepatitis, not nonalcoholic fatty liver. Brunt's classification evaluates steatosis, inflammation and ballooning.

questions.question: What histological stain is recommended to evaluate fibrosis in liver biopsy for non-alcoholic fatty liver?
questions.explanation: Masson's trichrome stains collagen blue/green, allowing liver fibrosis to be identified and quantified in the biopsy of patients with non-alcoholic fatty liver.

questions.question: What is the minimum size of the liver biopsy considered adequate to diagnose nonalcoholic fatty liver?
questions.explanation: A biopsy of at least 20 mm in length and 1.4 mm in diameter with at least 10 portal spaces is recommended for a representative histological evaluation of nonalcoholic fatty liver.

questions.question: What type of steatosis is most common in liver biopsy of non-alcoholic fatty liver?
questions.explanation: Perivenular macrovesicular steatosis (zone 3) is the most common pattern in nonalcoholic fatty liver, with large fat droplets that displace the nucleus to the periphery of the hepatocyte.

questions.question: What variables does the NAFLD fibrosis index (NFS) include?
questions.explanation: NFS includes age, BMI, platelets, albumin, AST/ALT, and fasting blood glucose; Option 1 contains the main variables and is correct.

questions.question: What is the NFS value that suggests advanced fibrosis?
questions.explanation: An NFS greater than 0.676 indicates advanced fibrosis (F3-F4); Values ​​between -1.455 and 0.675 are indeterminate and values ​​less than -1.455 rule out advanced fibrosis.

questions.question: What NFS parameter is obtained from blood biometry?
questions.explanation: The NFS uses platelet count (at 10^9/L) as a variable; The other options are not part of the index, although they are hematological parameters.

questions.question: What metabolic alteration is associated with elevated NFS?
questions.explanation: Impaired fasting blood glucose or diabetes mellitus is a variable of NFS; The other electrolyte alterations are not part of the calculation nor are they directly associated.

questions.question: What is the clinical use of NFS in NAFLD?
questions.explanation: The NFS is a non-invasive score to estimate the risk of advanced fibrosis in NAFLD; it does not diagnose steatohepatitis nor quantify steatosis directly.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of NFS?
questions.explanation: Total bilirubin is not part of the NFS; Its components are age, BMI, platelets, albumin, AST/ALT ratio and fasting blood glucose.

questions.question: What is the central pathophysiological mechanism linking insulin resistance and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease?
questions.explanation: Insulin resistance increases lipolysis in adipose tissue, releasing free fatty acids that accumulate in the liver, promoting hepatic steatosis.

questions.question: Which component of the metabolic syndrome is most strongly associated with the development of nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH)?
questions.explanation: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, by exacerbating insulin resistance and oxidative stress, is the factor that most correlates with the progression from simple steatosis to NASH.

questions.question: What is the first-line imaging test to diagnose hepatic steatosis in a patient with metabolic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is the recommended initial test due to its low cost, availability, and ability to detect hepatic steatosis non-invasively.

questions.question: What is the first therapeutic measure recommended in patients with non-alcoholic fatty liver associated with metabolic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Weight loss of 7-10% is the most effective initial intervention, reducing hepatic steatosis and improving insulin resistance.

questions.question: Which laboratory marker is typically elevated in patients with nonalcoholic fatty liver and metabolic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Alanine aminotransferase (ALT) is the liver enzyme that is most elevated in simple steatosis and NASH, reflecting hepatocellular damage.

questions.question: What metabolic alteration characterizes metabolic syndrome and directly contributes to hepatic steatosis?
questions.explanation: Compensatory hyperinsulinemia, secondary to insulin resistance, stimulates de novo lipogenesis in the liver, accumulating triglycerides in hepatocytes.

questions.question: What is the typical pattern of transaminase elevation in non-alcoholic steatohepatitis?
questions.explanation: In nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), ALT is usually higher than AST, unlike alcoholic hepatitis where AST predominates.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding suggests progression to hepatic fibrosis in fatty liver?
questions.explanation: When AST exceeds ALT in nonalcoholic fatty liver, it suggests advanced liver fibrosis, since AST is elevated due to mitochondrial damage.

questions.question: What is the first line of treatment to raise transaminases due to fatty liver?
questions.explanation: Weight loss through diet and exercise is the most effective initial intervention to reduce transaminases in nonalcoholic fatty liver disease.

questions.question: What drug is considered in adults with biopsy-proven NASH?
questions.explanation: Vitamin E (800 IU/day) is indicated in non-diabetic adults with non-alcoholic steatohepatitis confirmed by biopsy, due to its antioxidant effect.

questions.question: Which non-invasive test is most useful to evaluate fibrosis in fatty liver?
questions.explanation: Liver elastography measures liver stiffness and is the most validated non-invasive tool for estimating fibrosis in nonalcoholic fatty liver disease.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of ALT elevation in asymptomatic patients?
questions.explanation: Nonalcoholic fatty liver is the most common cause of mild ALT elevation in asymptomatic patients in Western countries, associated with obesity and insulin resistance.

questions.question: What is the typical value of the AST/ALT ratio in simple hepatic steatosis?
questions.explanation: In simple hepatic steatosis (NAFLD) the AST/ALT ratio is usually less than 1, reflecting a predominance of ALT over AST, unlike alcoholic hepatitis where it is usually greater than 2.

questions.question: Which liver enzyme pattern suggests advanced non-alcoholic steatohepatitis?
questions.explanation: In non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) with advanced fibrosis, the AST/ALT ratio can exceed 1, indicating greater mitochondrial damage and disease progression, although initially it is usually less than 1.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism that explains the elevation of ALT in NAFLD?
questions.explanation: Mitochondrial damage in NAFLD releases ALT from the hepatocyte cytosol, elevating its serum levels. ALT is more specific for liver damage than AST, and its initial predominance is associated with simple steatosis.

questions.question: What clinical condition is associated with an AST/ALT ratio greater than 2?
questions.explanation: In alcoholic hepatitis the AST/ALT ratio is usually greater than 2 due to pyridoxal phosphate depletion that reduces ALT activity, while in NAFLD the typical ratio is less than 1.

questions.question: Which liver enzyme is predominantly elevated in NAFLD without fibrosis?
questions.explanation: In NAFLD without fibrosis, ALT (alanine aminotransferase) is usually the highest enzyme, contributing to the AST/ALT ratio being less than 1, unlike alcoholic hepatitis where AST predominates.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding supports the diagnosis of NASH rather than simple steatosis?
questions.explanation: In NASH with fibrosis, the AST/ALT ratio can exceed 1, indicating greater necroinflammatory activity and mitochondrial damage, while in simple steatosis the ratio remains less than 1.

questions.question: What is the minimum recommended weight reduction to improve hepatic steatosis in NAFLD?
questions.explanation: A weight loss of 5-7% has been shown to significantly reduce hepatic steatosis in patients with nonalcoholic fatty liver, while smaller losses are insufficient.

questions.question: What type of exercise is recommended as a first line in the management of NAFLD?
questions.explanation: The combination of aerobic and resistance exercise is the most effective strategy to reduce liver fat content and improve insulin sensitivity in NAFLD.

questions.question: What is the primary mechanism by which weight loss improves NAFLD?
questions.explanation: Weight loss reduces insulin resistance, decreasing peripheral lipolysis and the flow of fatty acids to the liver, which improves steatosis and liver inflammation.

questions.question: Which diet is associated with greater effectiveness in reducing hepatic steatosis?
options: High protein diet without calorie restriction
questions.explanation: The low-calorie Mediterranean diet, rich in monounsaturated fats and antioxidants, has demonstrated superiority in reducing hepatic steatosis and improving metabolic parameters in NAFLD.

questions.question: What percentage of patients with NAFLD achieve resolution of steatohepatitis with weight loss ≥10%?
questions.explanation: Clinical studies have shown that weight loss of 10% or more is associated with resolution of steatohepatitis in approximately 90% of patients with NAFLD.

questions.question: What is the goal of weekly frequency of aerobic exercise in the management of NAFLD?
questions.explanation: At least 150 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise per week (60 minutes, 5 days per week) is recommended to achieve metabolic benefits and liver fat reduction in NAFLD.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of vitamin E in non-alcoholic steatohepatitis?
questions.explanation: Vitamin E acts as a fat-soluble antioxidant, reducing mitochondrial oxidative stress in hepatocytes, which improves non-alcoholic steatohepatitis.

questions.question: In which subgroup of patients with non-alcoholic steatohepatitis is vitamin E indicated as first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: The guidelines recommend vitamin E in non-diabetic adults with non-alcoholic steatohepatitis confirmed by biopsy, since in diabetics its benefit is not established and may increase risks.

questions.question: What is the recommended daily dose of vitamin E in the treatment of non-alcoholic steatohepatitis in adults?
questions.explanation: The standard dose of vitamin E for non-alcoholic steatohepatitis in adults is 800 IU per day, based on studies such as PIVENS that demonstrated histological improvement.

questions.question: What relative contraindication limits the use of vitamin E in patients with non-alcoholic steatohepatitis?
questions.explanation: Vitamin E has been associated with an increased risk of prostate cancer in men, so it is relatively contraindicated in patients with a history of this neoplasia.

questions.question: Which histological parameter significantly improves with vitamin E in non-alcoholic steatohepatitis?
questions.explanation: Vitamin E has been shown to reduce hepatocellular ballooning, a key component of necroinflammation in non-alcoholic steatohepatitis, according to controlled studies.

questions.question: What adverse effect has been associated with prolonged use of vitamin E in high doses?
options: Hemolysis in patients with vitamin K deficiency
questions.explanation: Vitamin E in high doses can antagonize vitamin K and cause hemolysis, especially in patients with underlying vitamin K deficiency or bleeding disorders.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of pioglitazone in type 2 diabetes?
questions.explanation: Pioglitazone is a PPAR-gamma receptor agonist, which improves insulin sensitivity in peripheral tissues, reducing blood glucose.

questions.question: What adverse effect is most frequently associated with the use of pioglitazone?
questions.explanation: Pioglitazone may cause fluid retention and edema, especially in patients with heart failure, due to its action on PPAR-gamma in the kidney.

questions.question: In which profile of patients with type 2 diabetes is pioglitazone indicated?
questions.explanation: Pioglitazone is indicated in patients with insulin resistance, as it improves peripheral sensitivity to insulin, reducing blood glucose.

questions.question: What cardiac contraindication limits the use of pioglitazone?
questions.explanation: Pioglitazone is contraindicated in heart failure (NYHA I-IV) due to the risk of exacerbation due to fluid retention and edema.

questions.question: What liver parameter should be monitored during treatment with pioglitazone?
questions.explanation: It is recommended to monitor hepatic transaminases (ALT/AST) before and during treatment with pioglitazone due to the risk of hepatotoxicity, although it is rare.

questions.question: What additional benefit does pioglitazone offer in nonalcoholic fatty liver?
questions.explanation: Pioglitazone reduces hepatic steatosis and inflammation in patients with nonalcoholic fatty liver by improving insulin sensitivity and decreasing lipogenesis.

questions.question: What is the main cause of death in patients with advanced nonalcoholic fatty liver?
questions.explanation: In patients with non-alcoholic steatohepatitis and advanced fibrosis, hepatocellular carcinoma is the most lethal complication, surpassing other hepatic and cardiovascular causes.

questions.question: Which noninvasive biomarker is associated with increased risk of cirrhosis in NAFLD?
questions.explanation: The FIB-4 index > 2.67 indicates advanced fibrosis and predicts cirrhosis in patients with NAFLD, being a validated non-invasive screening tool.

questions.question: What histologic feature distinguishes steatohepatitis from simple steatosis?
questions.explanation: Hepatocyte ballooning with lobular inflammation is the key finding that separates steatohepatitis (NASH) from simple steatosis, indicating active hepatocellular damage.

questions.question: What is the metabolic risk factor most strongly associated with cirrhosis in NAFLD?
questions.explanation: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is the most potent independent factor for progression to cirrhosis in NAFLD, due to insulin resistance and hepatic oxidative stress.

questions.question: Which imaging finding suggests established cirrhosis in NAFLD?
questions.explanation: Regenerative nodules on ultrasound or tomography indicate cirrhosis, as they reflect the liver architecture distorted by advanced fibrosis in NAFLD.

questions.question: What drug treatment reduces the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma in NAFLD?
questions.explanation: Vitamin E (tocopherol) has been shown to reduce steatohepatitis and the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma in non-diabetic NASH patients, although its use is controversial.
